museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: GoldenEye arrives, Final Fantasy VII is almost here in North America, and the N64 suddenly has a shooter nobody quite expected to become a social landmark.
museum.curatorNote: GoldenEye date references conflict by region and source, especially around Europe. This exhibit treats August as the verified Japan/North America arrival and the start of the phenomenon.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: A licensed game starts becoming something stranger and larger.

items.title: GoldenEye 007 launches in Japan and North America
items.body: Rare's N64 shooter arrived with modest expectations and began the slow transformation into one of the console's defining multiplayer objects.
items.sourceNote: Common references list Japan as August 23 and North America as August 25, 1997. European/UK date sources conflict, so no precise UK date is asserted here.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII nears Western shelves
items.body: Square's RPG was close enough that North American marketing and magazine attention made it feel present before the disc reached most players.
items.sourceNote: North American release follows in September 1997; Europe follows in November.

items.title: UK N64 owners get a new argument
items.body: Even where the local release date is contested by sources, GoldenEye's late-summer/late-1997 arrival became the game that changed the N64's reputation.
items.sourceNote: Some sources list Europe as August 25, while others list the UK as November 7. The exhibit avoids a fake exact UK date.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: August belongs to Bond, with Final Fantasy waiting just outside the door.

items.title: GoldenEye 007
items.body: Japan and North America release window for Rare's shooter, which would become the N64's social weapon.

items.title: Mario Kart 64
items.body: Still fresh in Europe, now joined by the idea that N64 multiplayer could mean shooting as well as racing.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII
items.body: A near-release presence in the West: previewed, advertised, and already mythic.
items.sourceNote: North American release follows in September.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: GoldenEye makes four controller ports feel less like a spec and more like a social contract.

items.title: Four controller ports
items.body: GoldenEye gave the N64's built-in multiplayer hardware a new identity: competitive, secretive, and endlessly replayed.
items.sourceNote: Derived from GoldenEye's multiplayer design and N64 hardware.

items.title: PlayStation memory-card seriousness
items.body: As Final Fantasy VII approached, the humble memory card began to feel more important. RPG time needed somewhere to live.
items.sourceNote: Final Fantasy VII Western release context.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The shelf starts dividing into Bond and Cloud.

items.title: GoldenEye coverage
items.headline: Bond becomes playable
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed N64 magazine-style cover image standing in for August 1997.
items.curatorNote: Before it became folklore, GoldenEye had to be explained as a licensed shooter that somehow worked.
items.body: Before it became folklore, GoldenEye had to be explained as a licensed shooter that somehow worked.

items.title: Final Fantasy VII countdown
items.headline: The RPG blockbuster approaches
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed PlayStation magazine-style cover image standing in for August 1997.
items.curatorNote: Screenshots of Cloud and Midgar made the PlayStation feel like a cinema machine.
items.body: Screenshots of Cloud and Midgar made the PlayStation feel like a cinema machine.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: GoldenEye was not online, but its reputation spread like it was.

items.title: Word of mouth did the network's job
items.body: GoldenEye's multiplayer stories moved through friends, shops, school, and magazines.

items.title: FFVII rumours and guides gathered early
items.body: Before many Western players owned it, Final Fantasy VII already had a trail of character names, screenshots, and speculation.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: August is the month the N64 learned a new tone.

items.title: Bond changed the room
items.body: The N64 was no longer just bright colours and analogue wonder. It could be tense, competitive, and a little adult.

items.title: PlayStation felt cinematic
items.body: Final Fantasy VII's approach made the console feel like it was about to host something larger than a game.

sources.label: GoldenEye 007 release context
sources.note: Used for Japan/North America dates and regional-date caution.

sources.label: Final Fantasy VII release context
sources.note: Used for September/November Western release build-up.
